overview: Cisco Software-Defined Access (SDA) is Cisco's SDN implementation for campus/enterprise LANs, combining a physical Underlay, a VXLAN Overlay, and DNA Center as the central controller.
definitions: Underlay: the physical network (routed access-layer switches) providing basic reachability.
Overlay: VXLAN tunnels carrying the actual endpoint data plane on top of the underlay.
Fabric: the combination of Underlay + Overlay.
LISP: the control-plane protocol SDA uses to discover endpoint locations.
RLOC (Routing Locator): the IP address of a fabric edge node in the underlay.
SGT (Scalable Group Tag): a 16-bit field (carried in VXLAN) used to apply security policy based on group membership instead of IP address.
core_concepts: DNA Center is the single central controller for SDA, and can also discover WLCs, unifying wired and wireless management.
SGT-based security lets policy be applied by group rather than by managing large IP-based ACLs.
protocol_operation: LISP resolves an endpoint's location to an RLOC in the underlay.
Traffic between fabric edge nodes is encapsulated in VXLAN across the underlay, carrying the endpoint's data plus its SGT.
packet_flow: Endpoint traffic enters a Fabric Edge node -> LISP resolves the destination endpoint's RLOC -> traffic is VXLAN-encapsulated (carrying SGT) -> forwarded across the underlay to the destination Fabric Edge -> de-encapsulated and delivered, with SGT-based policy applied.
header_fields: VXLAN header carries a 16-bit SGT field for group-based policy.
best_practices: Use SGT-based policy instead of large, IP-address-based ACLs when the design already runs on an SDA fabric.
security_notes: SGT lets security policy scale with group membership rather than IP addressing, greatly simplifying ACL management in large fabrics.
interview_questions: What are the three components of an SDA Fabric?
What problem does an SGT solve compared to traditional IP-based ACLs?
What role does LISP play in SDA?
exam_traps: Confusing NETCONF/RESTCONF (Southbound protocols DNA Center can use) with the SDA fabric's own data-plane protocol (VXLAN) and control-plane protocol (LISP).
enterprise_notes: SDA is the modern Cisco campus design pattern, replacing traditional VLAN-per-subnet designs with a fabric-based, policy-driven model.
